title: Ototoxic-medication awareness
description: Baked ototoxic-medication awareness dataset (issue #717) — surfaces a calm, informational note when an ACTIVE medication is a well-established ototoxic (hearing/balance-toxic) agent: an AMINOGLYCOSIDE antibiotic, PLATINUM chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin), a high-dose loop DIURETIC, a high-dose long-term SALICYLATE (aspirin), VANCOMYCIN, or QUININE / related antimalarials. Drugs match by RxNorm ingredient CUI + synonym (the shared machinery). INFORMATIONAL, never prescriptive — it never says stop a drug, never blocks anything, and the absence of a flag is NOT clearance (a curated subset). Fully OFFLINE. Committed + HUMAN-REVIEWABLE; regenerate with `npm run gen:ototoxic`.

citation.note: Uncopyrightable clinical facts (these drug classes are ototoxic) stated in our own words and cited to the ASHA consumer reference. Drug generic/brand names are public nomenclature.

citation.source: NIH NIDCD — Hearing, Ear Infections, and Deafness
citation.note: General NIH reference on hearing loss causes including medication-related (ototoxic) loss, stated in our own words.

entries.label: Aminoglycoside antibiotics (gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin)
entries.note: This is an aminoglycoside antibiotic, a class that can be toxic to the inner ear (hearing and balance) — sometimes permanently. Hearing/balance monitoring is sometimes advised during a course.

entries.label: Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, ethacrynic acid)
entries.note: This is a loop diuretic; at high or rapid intravenous doses it can affect hearing (usually reversibly), and the risk rises if it is taken alongside an aminoglycoside antibiotic.
entries.source: NIH NIDCD; American Speech-Language-Hearing Association — Ototoxic Medications

entries.label: Platinum chemotherapy (cisplatin, carboplatin)
entries.note: This is a platinum-based chemotherapy; cisplatin in particular is strongly toxic to hearing, and the loss can be permanent. Hearing is often monitored during treatment.

entries.label: Quinine and related antimalarials
entries.note: Quinine and related antimalarials can cause ringing in the ears and hearing loss; with quinine this is usually reversible, while the others rarely cause lasting loss.
entries.source: NIH NIDCD; American Speech-Language-Hearing Association — Ototoxic Medications

entries.label: Salicylates (high-dose aspirin)
entries.note: At HIGH doses taken over time, salicylates (aspirin) can cause ringing in the ears or temporary hearing loss that typically reverses when the dose is lowered or stopped. Low-dose (cardioprotective) aspirin is not generally implicated.
entries.source: NIH NIDCD; American Speech-Language-Hearing Association — Ototoxic Medications

entries.label: Vancomycin
entries.note: Vancomycin (a glycopeptide antibiotic) carries a risk of hearing effects, especially at high levels or when combined with an aminoglycoside; drug levels and hearing are sometimes monitored.
entries.source: NIH NIDCD; American Speech-Language-Hearing Association — Ototoxic Medications
